lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Ah, this is a wonderful area for a spot of hunting, don't you think?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I don't know, is it?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Oh absolutely; there's a huge variety of creatures to catch around here. You've got butterflies, polar kebbits, sabre-toothed kebbits, birds and even kyatts! Surely one of those piques your interest?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Where would I find polar kebbits?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Funnily enough, you've just put your finger right on the problem. With polar kebbits, finding them is the hardest part! You'll have to track them down.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How would I go about doing that, then?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: The easiest way to get started is by looking somewhere you can be sure a kebbit will have been and then working from there. You see the holes around here leading down into the ice? They're kebbit burrows!

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: You'll be hard pressed to catch a polar kebbit in its home, but if you search around the entrance to a burrow then you might be able to find a trail.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Now, kebbit trails are usually fairly easy to follow once you've found them, but if the trail does peter out, you should try inspecting the surrounding scenery for clues as to where the creature may have gone.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Good places to look for clues include hollow logs in which the kebbit may have hidden or vegetation which may have been chewed or damaged.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: There are also tunnels running through the ice here that the kebbits use, so you should check those out as well.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: What's more, these tunnels can often help to shelter a trail from further snow, making it even easier for you to pick them up again.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Eventually, of course, the kebbit will have probably hidden somewhere. Polar kebbits don't hang about in the open for long for fear of their natural predators.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: If you suspect a kebbit is close by, try looking around for signs of disturbed snow. Polar kebbits will often bury themselves in snow drifts as a means of hiding.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: If you think you've found a kebbit, use a noose wand to haul the creature out from the snow.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Wow, that's quite a lot to remember.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Don't worry; if you find yourself struggling to follow a trail, just come and ask me and I'll see if I can help.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: But remember, the best place to start is by searching around their burrows.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Great, thanks.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How do you hunt birds?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Ah, you can get cunning little bird snares for just that. You should be able to find them in specialist Hunter shops. Try to lay them in fairly open areas with lots of birds.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Do I need to place any bait on these traps?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: No, that's not necessary. The idea is that the bird will see the trap as somewhere to perch. If the bird lands on the trigger stick, it should drop away and a small loop of wire should close around the bird's feet.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: And remember, it's important to collect any captures from your traps fairly quickly; otherwise, you risk losing them to various wild scavengers.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How can I improve my chances of capturing a creature?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Ah, the key there is concealment, both of yourself and your traps.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: The most obvious point is to stay out of sight of your own traps. If possible, hide around a wall, hedge or similar. Failing that, make sure you're a good few feet away from your trap.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: You can hide yourself even better by wearing clothing appropriate to the area in which you are hunting.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Your best bet is to use the skins of creatures native to the area. There is a very good tailor in east Varrock who may be able to help you with making clothing out of such skins.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Finally, a lot of creatures may avoid your traps if they can smell a human scent on them. This is where smoking a trap can be useful.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: It takes a bit of practice, but by using the smoke from a torch, you can mask any other scents that may surround a trap.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Wow, thanks for your help.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Thanks for your help.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: You're welcome, good luck with the hunting.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What's so special about butterflies?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: That depends entirely on the butterfly.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: I'll admit that the activities of many butterflies may be of no interest at all. However, we are lucky in having around here some fine examples of rare thaumic butterflies.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: These butterflies reputedly have magical powers that, when released, can make a person stronger or heal otherwise critical wounds.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: At this point, I should offer a note of caution, however: it is also said that these powers will not manifest themselves if the butterflies are captured for selfish purposes.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Rather, they are gifts that must be used by one person to aid another. Of course, you'll have to catch them first.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: And how do I do that?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Why, with a butterfly net of course! You should also carry some empty butterfly jars with you in which to keep the butterflies.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How can I capture a sabre-toothed kebbit?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Ah, now for these creatures I would recommend using a deadfall trap. Deadfalls are quite easy to set up, and yet you can catch some very reasonably sized creatures with them.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Deadfall traps basically work by having a large dead weight, such as a boulder, positioned so as to be delicately balanced above some form of bait.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: You will need to make sure you have a knife and some wood with you to create appropriate supports and a trigger for this sort trap.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: In the case of sabre-toothed kebbits you should use some form of raw meat to bait your trap, but other animals may prefer other baits, such as fish or vegetables.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Oooh, kyatts sounds interesting. I want to catch one of those.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Well, you've set your sights high, kyatts are incredibly fearsome creatures, but with determination like that I'm sure you'll make it.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Now, you're not going to be able to catch a kyatt with just a small box or a noose on a stick. You need a much bigger trap.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Pit traps are a good bet. You'd be best off finding a natural dip in the ground as the basis for your trap. If you dig a pit yourself, you risk creatures noticing the disturbed ground nearby.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Once you've found an appropriate spot, you'll need some logs and a knife. The logs serve two purposes.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Some can be carved into sharp skewers for the bottom of the pit; hopefully the kyatt will die when it falls onto these. Others can be used to conceal the trap.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Of course, even then the pit is going to look suspicious, so you're going to need to lure the kyatt onto the branches in some way. What actually tends to work best for this is, well, yourself!

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What? I don't like the sound of that.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Yes, that's the normal response, but I kid you not. If you really want a kyatt to follow you over that pit then just give it a good poke!

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Of course, I suggest you use something long to poke it with. Poking a kyatt with your finger is likely to end in unfortunate limb loss.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Fortunately, you can get very good sticks for poking from most Hunter shops.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Once you have...got the creature's attention, try to lead it to one of your traps. Make sure to jump the branches when you reach the trap yourself, of course; it would be embarrassing to fall into your own trap.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Then, if the kyatt is giving you all of its attention, hopefully it won't notice the trap, and with any luck, it will stumble right into the pit.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That sounds a bit hit or miss, and kind of suicidal too!

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Well that's what makes it exciting, isn't it? But honestly, if you want to capture a kyatt, that's your best bet.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Can you teach me how to hunt?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Why certainly. I reached the peak of my abilities some time ago, but I'm more than happy to help newcomers to the field. Was there anything in particular you had in mind?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Where's the best place to start?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: I always say a good starting point is to learn how to follow the creatures you want to capture. Around here, that means practicing on polar kebbits.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So how do I track them down, then?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: The easiest way to get started is by looking somewhere you can be sure a kebbit will have been and then working from there. You see the holes around here leading down into the ice? They're kebbit burrows!

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: You'll be hard pressed to catch a polar kebbit in its home, but if you search around the entrance to a burrow then you might be able to find a trail.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Now, kebbit trails are usually fairly easy to follow once you've found them, but if the trail does peter out, you should try inspecting the surrounding scenery for clues as to where the creature may have gone.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What sort of creatures are there to hunt around here?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Well, there's pretty much anything you could want to hunt. You've got butterflies, polar kebbits, sabre-toothed kebbits, birds and even kyatts! Surely one of those interests you?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Sorry, I'm not that keen on hunting.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Really? What a shame, you looked like someone who could have had some promise.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Can you give me some hunting traps?

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: I do have a spare Hunter kit lying around, you can have it for free.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Thanks! I will put this to good use.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: I don't have any more Hunter kits lying around, sorry. Maybe you could try one of the many shops that sell hunting traps, you can find one in Yanille.

lines.speaker: Hunting expert
lines.text: Is there anything else I can help you with?
